speaker: interviewer
text: Henry Wermuth, and this is tape number six.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now after the last incident where I, well, was nearly shot-- nearly killed-- I said to my father, now we will not survive in this camp. Goeth will not have anybody survive whether they win or lose the war. We have to escape. And as I will show-- at the end, I will show this sketch map. But there is a part of the parameter of the-- of the camp was where us-- and mostly it's in a fence and another fence that's about a few doors, three meters, away. And then at one stretch of 30, 40 yards, the-- it will-- it run in one-- into one only. And we decided that's where we're going to escape. And we started to plan, to observe, and even a mock game develops among-- between my father and somebody else. I get it that I wasn't a better pupil at school to know my geography. But Switzerland was discussed, and in both cases, we would have to go either via Germany or via Czechoslovakia. And this-- but all the other places were taken by the Germans, occupied by the Germans. So while they played a game of geography, although my father didn't say it's the reason for escape, but, you know, how to plan a route, and I kept quiet because I couldn't say much. But gen-- gradually, sort of we made a date. We would-- it would be Saturday night that we would escape. It would be a night shift at that Saturday. And it was Thursday or Friday before we went to work-- I came a bit later-- that I looked at the place where we're going to escape. It's not very far from the metal workshop, the fence. And I saw people were there unrolling coils of barbed wire. It was as if Goeth, or Goeth, discovered the weak point and made sure that it wouldn't happen. Now to speak a bit more about that camp, Plaszow, I would say that in my experience, I would answer very personal. It w-- I felt worse there than even in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you feel worse there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've only esh-- talked to you about one or two incidents, but there are more. Shortly after we arrived there, there was a manhunt going on. For one reason or another Goeth or Gate-- Goeth, Goeth wanted 150 people. And we-- I don't know why we knew they were going to be killed. And to help them round up these people, usually Jewish police had to help them. And I-- my father and I, we thought, well, the best place to hide is in the police barracks. So we went there and because-- the wife of Sygmund Rosenblum, who was my father's cousin, and he was at a Jewish police, she made sure that we were not thrown out. Everybody else who would try to get in would have been thrown out. And speaking of these manhunts, now at other times, there were manhunts for sm-- on small-- for small boys. And one of these manhunts is very vividly depicted in the film, where a small boy tried frantically to hide. And wherever he looked, somebody was already there. In the end, he ended up by hiding in the latrine. Now latrine was for me when I saw it first a real shock-- a long, long board, 15, 20 yards with one hole next to each other without any partitioning, any wall, or anything. And everybody has to do his business and nobody sort of was interested. [INAUDIBLE] what he did, and that was the norm, how to live. But for us, maybe these holes wasn't big enough to-- for a grown up person to hide, but it was a hiding place, and there was a hunt for children. One even heard sometimes that they were fighting for a place there. And this is just to speak about Plaszow and about when a manhunt-- hunt was going on. Usually when somebody escapes, it was the norm of about 50 people being taken to the killing hill. That has another-- had another name too, but I've forgotten. I think Austrian or [? maybe hill. ?] Anyway, and killed up there. And now before my-- the story, as I say, before the last story which I told you about when I was nearly killed by this commandant Goeth-- it was Goeth by the way, as my father look-- when he looked out the window, he saw him. there was an incident where off-- he was very proud of his-- of his workshop, the garage, because, because we know he was proud because everybody who was visiting him had to be shown to the garage. And, and one of these people working in the garage managed to tie himself on the lorry and anyway escaped. This person, by chance, I knew. I forgot his name now. In the book I call him Daniel because his name start with a D. But I met him after the war and learned about his escape, but that's not my story. Anyway, he was-- he succeeded, one of the few. But then, of course, Goeth, Goeth, Goeth heard it, he ordered 50 to be killed. Now usually the 50 being killed come from the group where the person worked, but we knew he would not take anybody from the garage. So this fellow came from Bochnia, but we actually came from the ammunition camp of Klaj, spelled Kel-- K-L-A-J. So we hoped that the connection between Bochnia wouldn't been-- us and Bochnia wouldn't have been known. Anyway, 50 were taken to the killing hill at that time. That was before the incident when he shot at me. And also out of the 50-- it was late in the evening. They were going to be-- they were killed that night, but they were not sort of buried yet. And during the night, one of them also managed to get up. He was wounded. And he came-- and they had to undress, so they came naked into the neighboring workshop. And of course, they gave him-- they helped him. So I'm not the only one. It must have happened-- things might-- that way must've happened more than once. Now that was another incident. Now I switch back to the point of escape where I had to tell my father something terrible has happened. The point where we want to escape has now been-- sort of another coil of wire has been placed in front of it. So we have to make another plan, my father said. Now further in this camp-- and I'm not speaking now in sequence because that's the way that my memory goes. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Further in this camp, once we were all called out-- alles raus auf den Appellplatz-- to go to the assembly place. We were wondering what is it about-- anymore shootings or anything. We were always apprehensive. But you well sort of stood there in rows of five. That was the given thing there, but quite a few-- I think the whole camp was present there. I estimated 10,000 and that is my estimate, but other-- some people said there were 20,000 in this camp. Now on the sketch map, there are not enough barracks to house that 20,000. It's, as I say, only a sketch map. But as we are waiting as so, I was near the front, and I saw like some kind of a contraption like a football sort of a, a goal. And the-- only higher, not as wide, with two hooks on top. And immediately, the idea shot through my brain, and I said, oh, two hangings. And now, as I described it in the book, must I be ashamed that at this moment I've said two hangings, but thank god it isn't me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a short while we waited there, and in front of us a line of SS men with their rifles in hand. And I thought to myself, my god, if they-- where are the heroes of old? Now we are 10,000. They were about 100 in all. Charge them. A few will be shot, but we, we just would overpower them. If somebody would go and initiate this attack, I certainly would go with him, and perhaps the others would come because there would be no alternative but to be killed and we, we-- win or be killed. But later, what happened a bit later, I didn't think at the time, but that was-- those were my thoughts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, very shortly after that, the officers came along, amongst them, Goeth, which was probably the tallest man in the camp, except one prisoner, who was perhaps as tall and bigger as him. Even the other SS men couldn't reach him. And but he was tall and big with it-- obese. And he said to-- let's start. And the young fellow was brought up, and he was-- his hands were tied behind his back. And I was near the front, and I heard him plead and say in German, [GERMAN]. I'm innocent. And we knew that wouldn't-- I mean, anything wouldn't sort of help. And there was suddenly this OD man. OD stands for the German word of [GERMAN], order, serving in the name of the order. I am not sure how to pro-- translate a German word. They called OD men, the pol-- the Jewish police. And it was Salz with the two stripes who had to hang the boy. And he had to do the job, and the pleading didn't help. No when he was hung up and a chair pus-- pushed away from underneath him, the rope broke. And for a second or two, I thought, remember that by international law if something like that happened, it's like God's judgment, and the men will be freed. But as I say, a second or two only, but it wouldn't happen here. And a man on his tummy and with the hands tied crawled towards Goeth and repeated his pleas of course, and he pushed him away with his boot. And then I heard him say to the OD man, Salz, if it happens again, you hang. Now the OD man, of course, doubled the rope, and the boy was duly hanged and died. But I thought to myself, it may be a, a longer or more lingering death because it wasn't like cutting in like a rope, which sort of usually breaks your neck, but it was a double. Anyway, the next one, prisoners, yeah, the reason for his hanging was said to be as he was whistling the [NON-ENGLISH]. And somebody else-- I'm sure we just-- either the film or somewhere else I heard they was whistling 'The Communist Internationale.' Whatever it was, it was for whistling that he was hanged. And the other person, whatever he did, I don't know. I only remember the name was given as [NON-ENGLISH]. And we had a friend-- my family had a friend of that name in Frankfurt. I looked at the man, but it wasn't him. Maybe a relation, maybe his brothers. But he already was dead, dead. He was-- his hands were tied around the back. I don't know how he killed himself, but he was dead. But since the judgement was to be hanged, he was hanged.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then to finish the macabre show, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a smaller SS man, and he was leaning back to, to look taller, you know? He leaned back so far that it really looked ridiculous. And then pulled-- nonchalantly, he pulled a, a revolver, and shot the two hanging people, a bullet, you know, under the chin. There was a jerk and a movement, and that was the end, surely. And as I say, a, a picture-- a suitable end to a macabre show. Now what comes next? The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] another time we were called. You know, at this time, actually, I, I, I frantically thought if I could write these things down, I wanted to start my book then-- a book then, a-- some kind of a diary. But I had no pen and no paper. But I remember that it took me afterwards over 40 years to write my book.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next thing, we-- I was called out again, but alone and had to work outside, outside the camp. Now I, I tell you one incident before that. Also alone, I was taken and we were-- we already knew what it-- the work was going to be. A train has arrived with building material, and we were called then to unload bricks. And some people are already were on the wagons and loaded five bricks onto the shoulders of everybody. And we had to run like a marathon about a kilometer on-- onwards to a certain spot where you had to put down the bricks. But I never reached that far because although there were every 50 or 100 yards somebody with whips-- and I'm not sure if they were Germans or they were Ukrainians. I didn't quite look at the uniforms. But the word schnell, schnell--- quick, on the double, fast-- was eventually picked up by the Ukrainians very quickly and sounded like the Germans. So they whipped preferably those with loads, and so they had to run. But there was a little hill, and I went up that hill and down the other side. While I was going down the other side, I couldn't be seen from the back. And the front, that next guard just whipped somebody, so he wouldn't just see me. He was busy. So I shed my load, threw it down, and went back. And I was always thinking running-- if I had to run, it's better without a load than with a load.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I reached the wagon again I tried to get up onto the wagon because I thought loading it onto others is easier than carrying it yourself. It didn't work. Then I tried to get some halves amongst them. And I was warned they're harder to carry-- you know, they'll fall-- they'll fall down easier. And, and when they fall, you have to pick them up and very often with the beating to go with it. So again five, and again, at the same spot, and then back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were so many of us, although some were exhausted and maybe that they didn't get up even if they were beaten. I'm not sure there were some killed-- probably were. But there were so many of us that the wagon was soon unloaded. And when I came back to the barracks out of breath, exhausted, and in a terrible state and thinking, what, you know, of [? saving ?] [? and seeing ?] the others. The fellow who was sitting next to my father-- a Viennese fellow-- sung a s-- you know, was singing a Viennese song. If somebody knows any Austrian or Viennese songs, this was a famous called fiacre song. A fiacre is like a taxi, but with horses. And, and I was thinking what, what a thin dividing line between laughter and, and death. Anyway, that's just one more incident from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And another time which I was called, and I had to work outside the camp. And we were only guarded-- we were about 50 people-- only guarded by three Ukrainians. And I thought, ah, now there's a way-- that would be a way of escaping. But how do I get my father to come with me? And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for a moment thinking and speaking also later to my father, if we escape the others will be killed. So he reminded me. Although we knew about 50 going to be killed, we applauded the ones that got away. Who is hit is hit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now a second day, I went out, and just to, you know, plan a little bit more. When I came back it was da-- it was sort of almost dark. It was only minutes away from complete darkness. We were always countered. We were a group of 50, and the Ukraine-- the last thing to do was for the Ukrainians to count. And they counted. We lined up always in fives, and they counted and counted. And it was 49. Panic. One had escaped. And we already-- I saw already myself being led to the famous killing hill.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at the same time, at the moment we arrived in that camp, I saw from the distance about four, five-- 400, 300, 400 yards away from us, not on the usual assembly place, on a different place, I saw lots and lots of people. I heard voices there. And I thought, what was going on there? What was going on? There's shouting there. And somebody shouted, alles auf den Appelplatz, everybody to the assembly place, although the assembly place where the two were hanged was a different, different place. Now we heard this shouting, and they counted again-- 49. And then I heard a, a voice also nearer, shouting, alles zum [GERMAN]. And I shouted, they have already gone to the assembly place. The person's already gone to [INAUDIBLE]. Let's go. And I grabbed one on the left, one on the right, and pulled forward. And all the others came with me. Auf, I said. Didn't you hear? On the assembly place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they let us go because for them, they couldn't-- I'm sure they couldn't care less whether there's one more, you know. And it would have taken away from the drinking time of the evening, I suppose, if they had to, to report somebody missing. Probably they shrugged their shoulders and that was it. I just hoped that was another successful escape. But I was going to do that, but then I get a shock when somebody else did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I came to the place where there were, I would say-- that's guesswork-- but 2,00 people lining up and marching in fives, as they usually do, in rows of fives, away from that place. I said, what's going on? And I thought-- I asked them. Well, they took a number of people there. I said, did they take anybody from the metal workshops? No, no, nobody from the metal workshops. I thought, well, that's important. Money [INAUDIBLE] for whatever you produce probably goes into the commandant's pockets. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anyway, I was told my father wasn't there. The next thing was I had to get out of that group. But how will I get out of that group? So-- well, again, I was thinking if the commandant needs people to shoot, he's not shy. He would shoot anyway. And what will he do with people at night? I couldn't make head or tail what it is. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said whatever it is, I mustn't go to the-- you know, to line up wherever those, those people went. And anyway, I lined up and marched forward, looking for an opportunity to get away.
